description: The bedrock of all mathematics: mathematical thinking, logic, set theory, proof methods, and the nature of mathematical language and number systems.

concepts.name: Mathematical Thinking
concepts.description: The disciplined cognitive approach of reasoning precisely about abstract structures, identifying patterns, and constructing valid arguments from given information.

concepts.name: Abstraction
concepts.description: The process of extracting essential properties of mathematical objects while ignoring irrelevant details, enabling the same reasoning to apply across many contexts.

concepts.name: Pattern Recognition
concepts.description: The ability to identify recurring structures, relationships, or regularities in mathematical objects and use them to predict or generalize.

concepts.name: Mathematical Problem Solving
concepts.description: The structured application of strategies — working backwards, drawing diagrams, reducing to simpler cases, and systematic trial — to resolve novel mathematical questions.

concepts.name: Problem-Solving Strategies
concepts.description: A repertoire of techniques including drawing diagrams, working backwards, finding a simpler related problem, looking for symmetry, and exhaustive case analysis.

concepts.name: Mathematical Modeling
concepts.description: The process of translating a real-world situation into mathematical language, solving the mathematical problem, and interpreting the solution back in context.

concepts.name: Generalization
concepts.description: The act of formulating a statement that holds for a broad class of cases, typically discovered by examining specific instances and identifying invariant structure.

concepts.name: Mathematical Language
concepts.description: The precise symbolic and verbal conventions used to express mathematical ideas unambiguously, including notation, terminology, and the grammar of mathematical writing.

concepts.name: Mathematical Notation
concepts.description: The standardized system of symbols (=, +, ×, ∈, ∑, ∫, etc.) used to represent mathematical objects and operations compactly and unambiguously.

concepts.name: Variable
concepts.description: A symbol representing an unspecified or changing quantity, serving as a placeholder in expressions and equations and enabling the formulation of general mathematical statements.

concepts.name: Mathematical Symbols
concepts.description: The collection of standardized symbols used in mathematics including operational symbols, relational symbols, set symbols, logical connectives, and quantifiers.

concepts.name: Reading Mathematics
concepts.description: The skill of parsing and interpreting formal mathematical statements, definitions, theorems, and proofs accurately.
references: Solow 2014 How to Read and Do Proofs

concepts.name: Mathematical Logic
concepts.description: The branch of mathematics studying the principles of valid reasoning, the structure of mathematical statements, and the formal rules governing deduction.

concepts.name: Proposition
concepts.description: A declarative statement that is either true or false (but not both), forming the basic building block of propositional logic.

concepts.name: Logical Connectives
concepts.description: Operators (¬, ∧, ∨, →, ↔) that combine propositions to form compound statements, each with a precise truth-functional meaning.

concepts.name: Truth Table
concepts.description: A tabular method for determining the truth value of a compound proposition for every possible combination of truth values of its component propositions.

concepts.name: Predicate Logic
concepts.description: An extension of propositional logic that introduces predicates, terms, and quantifiers to express statements about objects and their properties.
references: Enderton 2001 A Mathematical Introduction to Logic

concepts.name: Predicate
concepts.description: A statement containing one or more free variables that becomes a proposition when the variables are assigned specific values.

concepts.name: Quantifiers
concepts.description: Logical symbols ∀ (for all) and ∃ (there exists) that bind variables and assert properties about entire domains or the existence of particular elements.

concepts.name: Logical Equivalence
concepts.description: Two compound propositions are logically equivalent if they have identical truth values for every assignment of truth values to their component propositions.

concepts.name: Rules of Inference
concepts.description: Formally valid argument patterns (modus ponens, modus tollens, hypothetical syllogism, etc.) that allow drawing new conclusions from given premises.

concepts.name: Mathematical Proof
concepts.description: A finite sequence of logically valid steps that establishes the truth of a mathematical statement with certainty, starting from axioms or previously proven results.

concepts.name: Direct Proof
concepts.description: A proof that directly derives the conclusion from the hypothesis through a chain of logical deductions, without requiring assumptions of the negation.

concepts.name: Proof by Contradiction
concepts.description: A proof technique that assumes the negation of the statement to be proved and derives a contradiction, thereby establishing the original statement.

concepts.name: Proof by Contrapositive
concepts.description: A proof of P→Q by instead proving ¬Q→¬P, exploiting the logical equivalence of a conditional and its contrapositive.

concepts.name: Mathematical Induction
concepts.description: A proof technique for statements indexed by natural numbers: establish a base case, then show that truth for n implies truth for n+1.

concepts.name: Strong Induction
concepts.description: A variant of induction in which the inductive step may use the truth of the statement for all values up to n, not just n alone.

concepts.name: Well-Ordering Principle
concepts.description: The principle that every nonempty subset of the natural numbers has a least element, equivalent to mathematical induction over ℕ.

concepts.name: Proof by Cases
concepts.description: A proof that partitions all possible situations into finitely many cases and proves the statement separately for each case.

concepts.name: Existence Proof
concepts.description: A proof that at least one object satisfying a given property exists, either by explicitly constructing it or by showing non-existence of its negation leads to contradiction.

concepts.name: Uniqueness Proof
concepts.description: A proof that demonstrates exactly one object satisfies a given property, typically by assuming two such objects exist and showing they must be equal.

concepts.name: Writing Mathematics
concepts.description: The craft of expressing mathematical reasoning clearly, precisely, and in standard notation, balancing rigor with readability.

concepts.name: Axiom
concepts.description: A statement accepted as true without proof, forming the foundational assumptions of a mathematical theory from which all other results are deduced.

concepts.name: Theorem
concepts.description: A mathematical statement that has been rigorously proved to be true from axioms and previously established theorems.

concepts.name: Lemma
concepts.description: A proven statement used as a stepping stone in the proof of a larger theorem, valued for its utility in breaking complex proofs into manageable parts.

concepts.name: Corollary
concepts.description: A result that follows easily from a theorem, often requiring only a brief additional argument or a direct substitution.

concepts.name: Conjecture
concepts.description: A mathematical statement believed to be true on the basis of evidence or intuition but not yet proven or disproven.

concepts.name: Mathematical Definition
concepts.description: A precise, unambiguous specification of the meaning of a mathematical term, introducing new vocabulary by relating it to previously defined concepts.

concepts.name: Axiomatic System
concepts.description: A collection of axioms together with the rules of logic from which all theorems of a mathematical theory are derived.

concepts.name: Set Theory
concepts.description: The mathematical theory of collections of objects (sets), providing the foundational language and framework in which all of modern mathematics is expressed.

concepts.name: Set
concepts.description: A well-defined, unordered collection of distinct objects called elements, the primitive object of set theory.

concepts.name: Set Membership
concepts.description: The fundamental binary relation ∈ asserting that an object x is an element of a set A, written x ∈ A.

concepts.name: Empty Set
concepts.description: The unique set containing no elements, denoted ∅ or {}, a subset of every set.

concepts.name: Set-Builder Notation
concepts.description: A notation {x ∈ A : P(x)} defining a set by specifying a domain and a predicate that its elements must satisfy.

concepts.name: Subset
concepts.description: A set A is a subset of B (A ⊆ B) if every element of A is also an element of B.

concepts.name: Proper Subset
concepts.description: A set A is a proper subset of B if A ⊆ B and A ≠ B, i.e., B contains at least one element not in A.

concepts.name: Set Equality
concepts.description: Two sets A and B are equal (A = B) if and only if they have exactly the same elements: A ⊆ B and B ⊆ A.

concepts.name: Set Operations
concepts.description: The standard operations on sets — union (∪), intersection (∩), difference (\), and complement (Aᶜ) — forming a Boolean algebra.

concepts.name: Union
concepts.description: The union A ∪ B is the set of all elements belonging to A, to B, or to both.

concepts.name: Intersection
concepts.description: The intersection A ∩ B is the set of elements belonging to both A and B.

concepts.name: Set Difference
concepts.description: The set difference A \ B consists of all elements in A that are not in B.

concepts.name: Set Complement
concepts.description: The complement of A relative to a universal set U is the set of all elements of U not in A.

concepts.name: Venn Diagram
concepts.description: A visual representation of sets and their relationships using overlapping circles within a universal rectangle.

concepts.name: Power Set
concepts.description: The power set P(A) is the set of all subsets of A; if A has n elements, then P(A) has 2ⁿ elements.

concepts.name: Cartesian Product
concepts.description: The Cartesian product A × B is the set of all ordered pairs (a, b) where a ∈ A and b ∈ B.

concepts.name: Ordered Pair
concepts.description: An ordered pair (a, b) is a pair of elements where the order matters: (a, b) = (c, d) iff a = c and b = d.

concepts.name: Relation
concepts.description: A relation R from A to B is any subset of A × B; the relation holds between a and b iff (a, b) ∈ R.

concepts.name: Reflexive Relation
concepts.description: A relation R on A is reflexive if (a, a) ∈ R for every a ∈ A.

concepts.name: Symmetric Relation
concepts.description: A relation R on A is symmetric if (a, b) ∈ R implies (b, a) ∈ R for all a, b ∈ A.

concepts.name: Transitive Relation
concepts.description: A relation R on A is transitive if (a, b) ∈ R and (b, c) ∈ R implies (a, c) ∈ R.

concepts.name: Equivalence Relation
concepts.description: A relation that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive, partitioning a set into disjoint equivalence classes.

concepts.name: Equivalence Class
concepts.description: The equivalence class [a] of an element a is the set of all elements related to a under the equivalence relation.

concepts.name: Partition
concepts.description: A partition of a set A is a collection of nonempty, pairwise disjoint subsets of A whose union equals A.

concepts.name: Partial Order
concepts.description: A reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive relation on a set, providing a generalized notion of ordering where not all pairs need be comparable.
references: Davey & Priestley 2002 Introduction to Lattices and Order

concepts.name: Total Order
concepts.description: A partial order in which every pair of elements is comparable: for all a, b, either a ⊴ b or b ⊴ a.

concepts.name: Hasse Diagram
concepts.description: A graphical representation of a finite partially ordered set where directed edges represent immediate predecessor relationships.

concepts.name: Function (Set-Theoretic)
concepts.description: A function f: A → B is a relation where every element of A is related to exactly one element of B, i.e., a right-unique and left-total relation.

concepts.name: Cardinality
concepts.description: The number of elements in a set; for infinite sets, the size is measured via bijections and gives rise to the theory of infinite cardinal numbers.

concepts.name: Finite Set
concepts.description: A set whose elements can be counted using a finite natural number; equivalently, one that is in bijection with {1, 2, …, n} for some n ∈ ℕ.

concepts.name: Countable Set
concepts.description: A set that is either finite or can be placed in bijection with the natural numbers ℕ; examples include ℤ and ℚ.

concepts.name: Uncountable Set
concepts.description: A set whose cardinality is strictly greater than that of ℕ; the real numbers ℝ are uncountable (Cantor's diagonal argument).

concepts.name: Axiomatic Set Theory
concepts.description: The standard axiomatic foundation of mathematics, ZFC, consisting of Zermelo-Fraenkel axioms plus the Axiom of Choice, resolving the paradoxes of naive set theory.

concepts.name: Ordinal Number
concepts.description: A generalization of natural numbers to describe the order type of well-ordered sets, extending counting into the transfinite.

concepts.name: Cardinal Arithmetic
concepts.description: The extension of arithmetic operations to infinite cardinals, including ℵ₀, ℵ₁, and the continuum hypothesis.

concepts.name: Inductive Reasoning
concepts.description: Reasoning from specific observations to general conclusions; in mathematics, this is conjectural and not logically certain (distinguished from mathematical induction, a proof technique).

concepts.name: Deductive Reasoning
concepts.description: Reasoning from general principles to specific conclusions in a logically valid manner; the mode of mathematical proof.

concepts.name: Natural Numbers
concepts.description: The set ℕ = {0, 1, 2, 3, …} (or {1, 2, 3, …}) with its canonical order and arithmetic operations, characterized axiomatically by the Peano axioms.

concepts.name: Integers
concepts.description: The set ℤ = {…, −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, …} extending the natural numbers to include additive inverses, forming a ring under addition and multiplication.

concepts.name: Rational Numbers
concepts.description: The set ℚ of all fractions p/q where p ∈ ℤ and q ∈ ℤ\{0}, forming a field and a dense total order without the completeness of ℝ.

concepts.name: Irrational Numbers
concepts.description: Real numbers that cannot be expressed as p/q with p, q ∈ ℤ; their decimal expansions are non-repeating and non-terminating (e.g., √2, π, e).

concepts.name: Real Numbers
concepts.description: The complete ordered field ℝ encompassing all rational and irrational numbers, characterized by the least upper bound (completeness) property.

concepts.name: Complex Numbers
concepts.description: The field ℂ = {a + bi : a, b ∈ ℝ} where i² = −1, completing the real numbers algebraically (Fundamental Theorem of Algebra) and enabling rotation in the plane.
